blocks.text: **Software** is the set of instructions that tells hardware what to do. Without software, hardware is just an expensive paperweight. Software is broadly divided into **system software** (manages the hardware) and **application software** (solves user problems).
System software manages and controls the computer hardware so that application software can function. The most important piece of system software is the **Operating System (OS)**.

blocks.definition: Software that manages hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage, I/O) and provides a platform for application software to run. Examples: **Windows**, **macOS**, **Linux**, **Android**, **iOS**.

items: **Process Management** — Schedules which program uses the CPU and when
**Memory Management** — Allocates RAM to processes, handles virtual memory
**File System Management** — Organizes data into files and folders on storage devices
**Device Drivers** — Provides standardized interfaces for hardware (keyboard, GPU, WiFi)
**Security & Access Control** — User accounts, permissions, sandboxing
**User Interface** — GUI (graphical) or CLI (command line) for interaction

blocks.text: Application software runs on top of the OS to help users accomplish specific tasks. These are the programs you use every day.

items.label: Browsers
items.desc: Chrome, Firefox, Safari — render web pages

items.label: Development Tools
items.desc: VS Code, IntelliJ IDEA, compilers, debuggers

items.label: Games
items.desc: Complex applications that push CPU/GPU to the limit

items.label: Databases
items.desc: PostgreSQL, MySQL — store and query structured data

blocks.text: You write code in a **high-level language** (Python, Java, C++) that humans can read. But CPUs only understand **machine code** — sequences of 0s and 1s. Something must bridge this gap.

tabs.label: Compiled Languages
tabs.content: A **compiler** translates your entire source code to machine code **before** execution. The result is a binary executable. Examples: **C, C++, Rust, Go**. Compiled programs are typically very fast because the translation overhead happens once.

tabs.label: Interpreted Languages
tabs.content: An **interpreter** reads and executes source code **line by line** at runtime. No separate compilation step needed. Examples: **Python, Ruby, JavaScript (traditionally)**. Easier to develop and debug but historically slower.
tabs.code:
# Python — interpreted line by line
print("Hello, World!")

tabs.label: Bytecode (JVM/JIT)
tabs.content: Languages like **Java** and **C#** compile to an intermediate **bytecode** that runs on a virtual machine (JVM). A **Just-In-Time (JIT)** compiler then compiles hot bytecode paths to native machine code at runtime — combining portability with near-native speed.

blocks.title: Abstraction Layers
blocks.text: Modern computing uses layers of abstraction: **Hardware → Firmware/BIOS → OS Kernel → System Libraries → Runtime → Application**. Each layer hides complexity from the layer above it.

points: Software is divided into system software (OS, drivers) and application software
The OS manages CPU time, memory, files, devices, and security
Compiled languages translate to machine code ahead of time; interpreted languages translate at runtime
Java and similar languages use bytecode + JIT compilation for portability and speed
Abstraction layers hide hardware complexity from application developers

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of system software?
questions.explanation: The Linux Kernel is system software — it directly manages hardware resources. Word, Chrome, and Spotify are application software that run on top of the OS.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
options: Compilers are faster at runtime; interpreters translate the whole program first
Compilers translate the entire program before execution; interpreters execute line by line
Interpreters only work with C++; compilers work with Python
questions.explanation: A compiler translates the complete source code to machine code before execution. An interpreter reads and executes source code line by line during runtime.
